original_abstract: This volume offers a comparative analysis of the political, military, and economic dimensions of Soviet occupation in Austria, Hungary, and Romania. Drawing on extensive evidence from Austrian, Hungarian, Romanian, and Russian archives, the authors challenge prevailing interpretations of Soviet foreign policy. While traditional scholarship has primarily investigated the political motivations behind Stalin’s decision to impose Bolshevik systems within the Soviet sphere of influence, this book argues for a broader understanding of his objectives. It demonstrates that the conquest of East-Central Europe was fundamentally imperial in nature, transforming occupied territories into strategic military and economic assets for the Soviet Union. Finally, the study examines the harrowing human cost of this era, documenting the widespread atrocities, plunder, and forced deportations that defined the daily reality of those living under Soviet rule.
humanized_abstract: This book offers the first comparative history of the political, military and economic aspects of the Soviet occupation of Austria, Hungary and Romania. Drawing on a wide range of primary sources in five languages, including materials from the Austrian, Hungarian, Romanian and Russian archives, the authors of the twelve chapters not only challenge recent interpretations of Soviet behaviour in the occupied countries but also illuminate in unprecedented detail the motivations of Stalin and other Soviet leaders. A new perspective on Stalin's conquest of Eastern Europe is provided, highlighting the imperial and economic dimensions of his policies. This involved transforming the conquered countries into regions of Soviet military and economic power. Finally, the volume casts a light into some of the darkest corners of post-war history, such as massacres, looting and deportations, and highlights the awful experiences of people living under Soviet occupation.
